input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system is a laboratory‑scale gantry crane used as an exemplar for digital twin research. It consists of a small‑scale mechanical gantry structure, a moving carriage, a hoist mechanism, and an electronic controller that drives the crane’s motions. The crane operates in a laboratory environment, is modular and inexpensive, and relies on open‑source, freely available hardware and software, making it suitable for reproducible experiments and education.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin provides several services built on CAD and kinematic models of the crane: (1) an optimal‑control service that computes reference trajectories and control commands to improve crane performance; (2) a historical‑data‑logging service that records sensor and operational data for offline analysis; (3) a data‑visualisation service that renders real‑time and recorded crane states; and (4) a continuous‑validation service that automatically compares live sensor data with the predictive model to detect deviations. These services target researchers and educators who use the twin to test control algorithms, fault‑detection methods, or system‑evolution notations.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The twin is implemented using a containerised architecture built with Docker. Core components include a TimescaleDB time‑series database for logging, an Eclipse Mosquitto MQTT broker for real‑time data exchange, and a Grafana dashboard for visualisation. Service logic is programmed in Python and runs on an Arduino‑based controller that interfaces with the crane motors and sensors. The digital side’s CAD and kinematic models are created with Autodesk Fusion360. All software components are open‑source and orchestrated as C4‑style containers to provide modular, reproducible deployment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The exemplar adopts an ad‑hoc, container‑based orchestration that follows the C4 modelling approach. A set of Docker containers implement the core services: a Python logging application that continuously records the physical system’s state, an MQTT broker (or ActiveMQ in the greenhouse case) that provides publish/subscribe signalling between services, a time‑series database (InfluxDB) for persisting sensor data, and the digital‑twin logic itself (implemented as a Python module). Deployment is driven by a docker‑compose file that specifies container images, network links, and resource limits, allowing the whole constellation to be started, stopped and scaled with a single command. Coordination among the components is realised through MQTT topics: the physical system publishes sensor readings, the twin consumes them to update its model, and control commands are sent back on dedicated topics. The architecture is deliberately non‑framework‑centric, relying on widely used open‑source tools (Docker, MQTT, InfluxDB, ActiveMQ) to manage resources, handle inter‑service communication and maintain system‑wide coherence.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: GreenhouseDT incorporates a digital twin that includes behavioral adaptation models of the greenhouse and stores time‑series sensor data in an InfluxDB database; communication between components is handled via an ActiveMQ message broker and the system is packaged in virtual machines containing prerecorded data. The Air Quality Management exemplar uses an AutomationML‑based model to represent the indoor environment, acquires measurements from a Raspberry‑Pi equipped with sensors, and processes the data through a Microsoft Azure‑based pipeline.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The system includes a Logging Application that continuously logs the crane’s state to a TimescaleDB database. Measurements of executed trajectories are also supplied to the Continuous Validation Service, which compares them with simulated trajectories. Data flow is performed by the Python‑based Physical Crane Interface which reads sensor values from the crane controllers and forwards them to the logging component; the communication mechanism (protocol, frequency, preprocessing) is not explicitly detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control of the crane is performed by a Python Control Application that uses the Physical Crane Interface to send commands to the crane’s controllers. The Trajectory Generator Service produces optimal movement trajectories (kinematic model) that are delivered to the crane via this interface. No specific command formats, validation, authentication, or execution‑confirmation mechanisms are described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Inter‑service communication within the digital twin relies on an MQTT broker that provides publish/subscribe signaling between the Trajectory Generator Service, Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service, and other components. The broker is the only explicitly mentioned network protocol; the document does not describe network topology, bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security measures for the MQTT connection.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: All software components (Physical Crane Interface, Control Application, Logging Application, Human‑Machine Interface, Database, Dashboard, Trajectory Generator Service, Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service, and MQTT Broker) are executed on a single personal computer. The Database, MQTT Broker, and Grafana Dashboard are packaged as Docker containers, and the system can be started with a Docker‑Compose configuration. No explicit CPU, memory, or storage requirements are given, and the document does not mention cloud or edge hosting, scalability beyond container replication, or formal availability guarantees.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twins provide runtime visualisation dashboards, simulation modules and prediction services (e.g., for indoor air‑quality) that enable operators to view current sensor values, forecast future conditions and evaluate control actions. These capabilities are accessed through web‑based interfaces, but the documents do not specify the underlying analytics algorithms, confidence measures, or detailed decision‑making workflows.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The gantry crane digital twin uses the C4 Model to visualize and describe its architecture, applying a software‑architectural notation to the cyber‑physical system.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the system as a lab-scale gantry crane for research and education, and lists main physical components like the structure, carriage, and hoist. It captures the system's modularity and reliance on open-source elements. However, it misses specific hardware details (e.g., specific motors, sensors like anemometers) and the claim that it is 'inexpensive' is slightly contradicted by the text stating it costs 'approximately 1250 Euro' and is 'not low cost'. Supported by: 'lab-scale gantry crane', 'physical crane and its controller', 'used for future research or education'.

The extraction provides a highly detailed and accurate summary of the digital twin's services, matching the source text closely. It lists the trajectory generation (optimal control), logging, visualization, and continuous validation services with their specific functions. It also correctly identifies the target users and the specific applications (fault detection, system evolution) mentioned in the text. Supported by: 'services for optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization and continuous validation', 'Trajectory Generator Service... generate optimal movement trajectories', 'Continuous Validation Service... continuously compares measurements'.

The description correctly identifies the containerized architecture using Docker: 'containerized with Docker.' It lists TimescaleDB as the database ('TimescaleDB database'), Eclipse Mosquitto as the MQTT broker ('Eclipse Mosquitto was used'), and Grafana for visualization ('Grafana dashboard'). It correctly identifies Python as the programming language for services ('bundled all the Python C4 Containers') and mentions the Arduino controller ('An Arduino measures and controls'). CAD models are attributed to Autodesk Fusion360 ('CAD models... made in Autodesk Fusion360'). All details are present in the source and roles are correctly assigned.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

the design and implementation of digital twins for smart manufacturing,”

block_2_judge.reasoning: The characteristic is correctly identified as 'Not in Document'. While the text mentions 'continuous validation' and 'time series data', it does not provide specific details on synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs as defined by the characteristic description.

The characteristic is correctly identified as 'Not in Document'. The text describes a single lab-scale gantry crane digital twin with a specific C4-based hierarchy of components. It does not discuss 'multiple digital twin instances', 'coordination mechanisms between twins', or a hierarchy of twins, other than mentioning a future teaching extension that was deemed immature.

The description is mostly accurate and well-supported by the text. It correctly identifies the C4 modeling approach, the use of Docker containers for orchestration (Database, MQTT Broker, Dashboard), and the ad-hoc, non-framework-centric architecture using open-source tools like MQTT and Python. However, it incorrectly specifies 'InfluxDB' as the time-series database, whereas the text explicitly states 'TimescaleDB' (based on PostgreSQL). This is a specific technical error.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The document focuses on an internal, ad-hoc setup for a specific exemplar and explicitly contrasts it with framework-based approaches. While it mentions standard software (MQTT, SQL), it does not describe integration with external enterprise systems, specific interoperability standards for external exchange, or dependencies on other Digital Twins. Therefore, the assessment is correct. Quotes: 'we utilize a more or less ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework', 'The exemplar... only relies on other freely available and commonly used software'.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

block_4_judge.reasoning: Accurately captures the data flow components and processes mentioned in the text. It correctly identifies the Logging Application, the Physical Crane Interface, and the Continuous Validation Service, along with their specific roles. It also correctly notes that specific communication protocols and frequencies are not detailed in the source. Supported by quotes: 'Logging Application: a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database', 'Physical Crane Interface: the interface to the physical crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the crane'.

Correctly describes the control flow, identifying the Control Application and Trajectory Generator Service as the sources of commands. It accurately specifies the use of the Physical Crane Interface for sending commands. It also correctly identifies the absence of specific command formats or validation mechanisms in the text. Supported by quotes: 'Control Application: a Python application that uses the crane interface to control the crane', 'Trajectory Generator Service: a service that utilizes a kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement trajectories for the crane'.

The extraction is precise regarding the inter-service communication architecture. It correctly identifies MQTT as the protocol used for signaling between services and explicitly mentions the use of the Eclipse Mosquitto broker. It is accurate in noting that the document does not provide details on network topology, bandwidth, or security measures.

The extraction provides a highly specific summary of the deployment strategy. It correctly states that all software runs on a 'single personal computer' and identifies which components are containerized with Docker (Database, MQTT Broker, Dashboard). It is also accurate in noting that specific hardware requirements and cloud hosting options are not discussed.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The document describes the development approach as 'ad-hoc' and discusses the system's usage in research and teaching, but it does not explicitly define lifecycle phases, representation types at different phases, transition management, or lifecycle governance approaches. Thus, the statement that this characteristic is 'Not in Document' is correct.

The document mentions 'DarTwin, a notation for system evolution' (Section I) and discusses evolution in the context of publications (Section IV.A), but does not describe engineering methodologies, quality assurance practices, or version control strategies. The extraction 'Not in Document' is accurate.

The extraction incorrectly attributes 'prediction services (e.g., for indoor air‑quality)' to the gantry crane digital twin. The source text associates indoor air quality prediction with a different exemplar (Air Quality Management) in the Related Work section. The gantry crane twin provides visualization, simulation, and validation, not air quality prediction. Supported by quote: 'The Air Quality Management exemplar was created... focuses on a construction engineering case of indoor air quality management... Three example applications... simulation and prediction.'

The document states, 'we use the C4 Model to visualize and describe the architecture of the system' (Section III.A), and notes that it is 'tailored for software architectures' but applied to the cyber-physical system. This is a specific standard adopted, but no other industry standards or compliance frameworks are mentioned. The extraction is accurate and specific.

_A. Architecture_

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.
